Title: Cytokine storm dynamics in hantavirus pulmonary syndrome: a multiscale ODE model with Wasserstein early-warning score and application to the 2026 Andes virus outbreak
Key summary: This mechanistic mathematical modelling study of Andes hantavirus infection demonstrates that, despite controlled viral replication, a self-sustaining immune feedback loop can drive a cytokine storm once a critical threshold is reached, with early warning signals detectable 1–2 days prior to clinical deterioration. It further proposes a six-variable patient stratification tool and identifies day-7 IL-10–based therapy as the most effective intervention for reducing severe vascular complications, outperforming corticosteroids and ECMO.

Title: International risk of secondary hantavirus clusters following MV Hondius outbreak
Key summary: This mathematical modelling study adapted a stratified branching-process model parameterized with country-level contact tracing and isolation data to estimate the likelihood of secondary transmission following exposure to Andes virus cases linked to the MV Hondius outbreak. The model suggests that overall transmission risk is low, but additional clusters remain possible under conditions of imperfect isolation or pre-symptomatic spread, supporting continued coordinated contact tracing during the incubation period.

Title: Transmission potential of Andes virus from human to human
Key summary: The study, which analyzed documented human-to-human transmission events of Andes virus (ANDV) from eight published studies between 1993 and 2019, estimates the basic reproduction number (*R*?) of ANDV at 0.24, indicating limited transmissibility and suggesting that sustained outbreaks are unlikely. Based on data from Argentina and Chile, the research also reveals over-dispersed transmission patterns, with some clusters involving more than 10 cases, highlighting the need for further investigation into environmental and epidemiological factors contributing to rare super-spreading events.

Title: Andes virus outbreak linked to expedition cruise ship travel, multi-country investigation and response, April to June 2026
Key summary: Using epidemiological and serological data as well as publicly available sequences, the authors describe that the Andes virus outbreak on the MV Hondius cruise ship likely began  with zoonotic infections before embarkation, followed by human-to-human transmission in confined spaces, resulting in a case fatality rate  of 23%  (3 deaths of 13 linked cases) and prompting a multinational epidemiological investigation. No new cases have been identified as at 18 June, and the quarantine period for most individuals is nearly complete. Ongoing community transmission is considered very unlikely as exposed contacts have been identified and quarantined with weekly testing and/or monitoring, and are rapidly tested if symptomatic.

Title: Polyfunctional antibody signature defines protection in Andes hantavirus survival
Key summary: The authors of this study performed a serological profile of acute hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) patients and survivors, analyzing antibody types, subtypes, and neutralization capacity targeting ANDV glycoproteins (Gn and GnGc). Reference: Fama, et al, (2026), Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases
Title: Andes virus-associated cardiopulmonary syndrome: diagnostic approaches, clinical management, and evidence gaps-a scoping review
Key summary: This scoping review maps evidence on diagnostic and clinical management strategies for Andes virus–associated hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (ANDV-HCPS), including 40 studies involving 3,500 confirmed patients across endemic and non-endemic regions. Serology served as the primary diagnostic modality in routine practice, with RT-PCR reserved for diagnostically challenging cases; however, no validated ANDV-specific point-of-care test was identified. Management was entirely supportive, with mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, and ECMO used in severe cases associated with improved survival in specialist centres. No approved antiviral therapy exists; evidence for corticosteroids and immune plasma remains limited and inconclusive.
Foci: Clinical Data; Diagnostic / Detection test performance

Reference: Lopez, et al, (2023), BMC Public Health
Title: Modeling potential risk areas of Orthohantavirus transmission in northwestern Argentina using an ecological niche approach
Key summary: The study developed an Orthohantavirus risk transmission map for Northwestern Argentina (NWA) using ecological niche models (ENM) of three rodent reservoir species. The resulting risk map highlighted the Yungas rainforest and parts of the Dry Chaco ecoregions as areas with the highest transmission risk, correlating with 512 and 231 HPS cases, respectively. They estimated that 18% of the NWA population was at risk and 78% of the cases occurred less than 10 km from deforestation. The highest niche overlap was between Calomys fecundus and Oligoryzomys chacoensis.
